Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → AI safety, ethics, and responsible AI → Explainability and interpretability

Lesson 2 of 3

Explainability and interpretability

SHAP, LIME, attention visualization, and model-agnostic explanation methods for building trust and debugging models.

25-35 min AI/ML flow AI safety, ethics, and responsible AI 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Stakeholders, regulators, and users need to understand why a model made a decision. Explainability enables trust, debugging, and compliance.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Explanation methods SHAP provides theoretically grounded feature importance with consistency guarantees2. Designing for interpretability Linear models and decision trees are inherently interpretable for simple problems3. Limitations and risks Post-hoc explanations may not reflect the actual decision process

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Stakeholders, regulators, and users need to understand why a model made a decision. Explainability enables trust, debugging, and compliance. 2. Explanation methods SHAP provides theoretically grounded feature importance with consistency guarantees3. Designing for interpretability Linear models and decision trees are inherently interpretable for simple problems4. Limitations and risks Post-hoc explanations may not reflect the actual decision process
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Stakeholders, regulators, and users need to understand why a model made a decision. Explainability enables trust, debugging, and compliance.

What to say first

  • Can select an appropriate explanation method for the model type and audience.
  • Understands the limitations and potential faithfulness issues of each method.
  • Designs explanation interfaces appropriate for the stakeholder audience.
2

Study section

Explanation methods

SHAP values, LIME, integrated gradients, and attention visualization provide different perspectives on model decisions.

Call these points out explicitly

  • SHAP provides theoretically grounded feature importance with consistency guarantees
  • LIME generates local explanations by approximating the model with interpretable surrogates
  • Attention weights provide some insight but are not always faithful explanations
3

Study section

Designing for interpretability

Choosing inherently interpretable models, constraining architectures, and building explanation interfaces for different audiences.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Linear models and decision trees are inherently interpretable for simple problems
  • Concept bottleneck models force intermediate representations to be human-understandable
  • Different audiences (engineers, executives, end users) need different explanation formats
4

Study section

Limitations and risks

Explanations can be misleading, unfaithful to the model, or provide false confidence in flawed systems.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Post-hoc explanations may not reflect the actual decision process
  • Users may over-trust systems that provide plausible but incorrect explanations
  • Explanation methods have their own failure modes and assumptions

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can select an appropriate explanation method for the model type and audience.
  • Understands the limitations and potential faithfulness issues of each method.
  • Designs explanation interfaces appropriate for the stakeholder audience.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Treating attention weights as definitive explanations of model behavior.
  • Providing explanations without validating their faithfulness to the model.
  • Using complex explanation methods when a simpler model would be more appropriate.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Compare SHAP and LIME. When would you use each?
  • A loan application was denied by your model. Design an explanation for the applicant.
  • How would you verify that an explanation method is faithful to the model behavior?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "Explainability and interpretability" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • Compare SHAP and LIME. When would you use each?
  • Explanation methods
  • Designing for interpretability
  • Can select an appropriate explanation method for the model type and audience.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants

Bring your own model
